comments.author: oompah
comments.text: UNDERSTANDING: The issue reports 'ApiAgentSession.run_task failed: timed out'. This error originates from api_agent.py:1221 in the  handler within . The  function at api_agent.py:637 uses  (5 min) for urllib.urlopen. When the endpoint is reachable but slow, or when there's a network-level timeout, urllib raises a URLError which gets wrapped in TransientServerError. The retry logic in  (lines 1263-1277) handles TransientServerError with exponential backoff, but the issue is that the outer exception handler in run_task (line 1218-1223) catches and returns immediately without allowing retries. The exception is being caught as a generic Exception rather than being re-raised for retry.

comments.author: oompah
comments.text: Discovery: Found the root cause in api_agent.py. The _http_post function uses timeout=300 for urlopen. When a timeout occurs, it raises URLError which is caught and wrapped in TransientServerError (line 1197). However, in _call_api, the retry loop only catches RateLimitError and TransientServerError from the await asyncio.to_thread call (lines 1248-1277), but the issue is that the timeout exception from urllib might be happening in a different context. Let me investigate the _call_api method more carefully.

comments.author: oompah
comments.text: Implementation: Increasing the HTTP timeout in api_agent.py _http_post from 300s (5 min) to 600s (10 min). Deep reasoning tasks often take longer than 5 minutes for a single API response. Also adding a test case for timeout handling.

comments.author: oompah
comments.text: Verification: All 44 tests in test_api_agent_budget.py pass (5 new tests for HTTP classification including test_timeout_error_raises_transient_server_error). The timeout is now 600s (was 300s), which gives deep-reasoning models more time to respond.

comments.author: oompah
comments.text: Completion: Changed _http_post timeout from 300s to 600s in oompah/api_agent.py. Deep-reasoning tasks can take longer than 5 minutes for a single API response. Added test to verify timeout errors are properly classified as TransientServerError (retryable). All tests pass (44 in test_api_agent_budget.py). Branch pushed.

title: [backend:api_agent] ApiAgentSession.run_task failed: HTTP 400 from http://100.64.0.3:8888/v1/chat/completions: {"error":{"message":"This model's maximum context length is 196608 tokens. However, yo...
description: ApiAgentSession.run_task failed: HTTP 400 from http://100.64.0.3:8888/v1/chat/completions: {"error":{"message":"This model's maximum context length is 196608 tokens. However, you requested 32768 output tokens and your prompt contains at least 163841 input tokens, for a total of at least 196609 tokens. Please reduce the length of the input prompt or the number of requested output tokens. (parameter=input_tokens, value=163841)","type":"BadRequestError","param":"input_tokens","code":400}}
close_reason: Fixed: ApiAgentSession now budgets each chat/completions call against the model's declared context window — prunes oldest assistant/tool round-trips in pairs and clamps max_tokens to fit. Opt-in per provider via model_contexts; set for Godspeed (nvidia/MiniMax-M2.7-NVFP4 → 196608).

comments.author: oompah
comments.text: Starting work. Root cause: oompah/api_agent.py:958 hardcodes max_tokens=32768 on every chat-completions call regardless of how full the conversation history is. Once messages cross (model_max_context - 32768) input tokens, the provider 400s with off-by-one overflow and every retry fails identically. Plan: (1) add per-model max_context to ModelProvider config; (2) before each _call_api, estimate the prompt's token count and (a) prune oldest non-system messages until it fits with a safety margin, (b) clamp max_tokens to the remaining budget. Approximation: tokens ≈ len(json.dumps(messages))/4 — good enough since we already need a safety margin for tokenizer drift.

comments.author: oompah
comments.text: Implementation done. Changes: (1) ModelProvider gained 'model_contexts: dict[str,int]' for per-model window size + a get_model_context(model) accessor. (2) api_agent.py gained two pure helpers — _estimate_tokens (4-chars-per-token rule + JSON serialization) and _prune_messages_to_fit (drops oldest assistant+tool-response groups in pairs, never touches system/initial-user, never orphans tool messages). (3) ApiAgentSession.__init__ takes a new model_max_context arg; _call_api budgets each call: prunes if needed, then clamps max_tokens to (window - est_input - safety_margin), floored at 1024 so the model can always reply. (4) Orchestrator threads provider.get_model_context(model) into the session. (5) .oompah/providers.json now declares model_contexts={'nvidia/MiniMax-M2.7-NVFP4': 196608} for Godspeed. Behavior is opt-in: if a provider doesn't set model_contexts, the legacy fixed max_tokens=32768 still applies. Added tests/test_api_agent_budget.py with 14 tests covering token estimation, pruning invariants (head preserved, tool/assistant pairing never broken, idempotence when already fits), session construction, and end-to-end _call_api behavior with the HTTP layer mocked. Full suite: 1141 passed (was 1127).

comments.author: oompah
comments.text: Verification: full test suite (1141 passed, was 1127 — 14 new tests cover token estimation, prune invariants, session construction, and end-to-end _call_api budget behavior with HTTP mocked). Service restarted (pid 56860); orchestrator picked up the new model_max_context wiring from .oompah/providers.json. Closing.

description:
Failed to fetch candidates: bd command failed (exit 1): Error: no beads database found
Hint: run 'bd where' to inspect the resolved workspace, or 'bd init' to create a new database
      or set BEADS_DIR to point to your .beads directory
close_reason: Mitigation landed: tracker.py TTL short-circuit + WARNING-level logging silences the auto-file loop. Underlying environmental issue (no beads DB at ~/.oompah/repos/trickle/.beads/) is now visible only as a single WARNING per minute in oompah.log; user can run 'bd init' there or remove the trickle project from .oompah/projects.json when convenient. No need to keep a bead open as a reminder.

comments.author: oompah
comments.text: Mitigation landed: tracker.py now raises TrackerNotConfiguredError (subclass of TrackerError) when bd reports 'no beads database found', and short-circuits subsequent calls for 60s via an instance-level cache. The error is logged once per TTL window at WARNING (not ERROR), so error_watcher no longer auto-files duplicate beads for it. 5 duplicate beads (2lh, 33g, 51f, jqw, y3n) superseded by this one. Underlying issue remains: ~/.oompah/repos/trickle/.beads/ exists but has no DB. To fully resolve, either run 'bd init' in the trickle workspace or remove the trickle project from .oompah/projects.json. Keeping this bead open as the tracking record for the environmental fix; the noise itself is gone.

title: Replace polling with event-driven calls
description: Replace all instances of polling with event-driven function calls
close_reason: Replaced polling with event-driven watchfiles.awatch() in LogFileWatcher. PR: https://github.com/lesserevil/oompah/pull/37

comments.author: oompah
comments.text: I understand the issue: Replace all polling patterns with event-driven function calls. After analyzing the codebase, I've found the main polling pattern is in LogFileWatcher (error_watcher.py) which polls a log file every 2 seconds using asyncio.sleep(). The fix is to replace this with watchfiles.awatch() which is already a dependency (used in __main__.py for workflow reloading) - this gives us inotify/FSEvents/kqueue-backed file watching instead of polling. Other 'while True' loops in the codebase (agent stderr drain, websocket message loop, etc.) are I/O event-driven reads, not polling, so they don't need changes.

comments.author: oompah
comments.text: I understand the issue from the previous agent's analysis. The main polling pattern to fix is in LogFileWatcher.start() in error_watcher.py - it uses asyncio.sleep(2.0) in a while loop to check for new log file content every 2 seconds. The fix is to replace this with watchfiles.awatch() (already in dependencies, already used in __main__.py) for event-driven file change notifications. The _poll_file() method and _seek_to_end() infrastructure can remain, but the polling loop gets replaced with an awatch event loop. My approach: (1) Replace the sleep-based loop in LogFileWatcher.start() with awatch(), (2) Keep backward-compat by removing poll_interval parameter or keeping it for fallback, (3) Update tests to cover the new event-driven behavior.

comments.author: oompah
comments.text: Implementation complete. Changed LogFileWatcher in oompah/error_watcher.py to use watchfiles.awatch() for event-driven file watching instead of asyncio.sleep() polling. Key changes: (1) Removed poll_interval parameter - no longer needed; (2) Added _stop_event: asyncio.Event for clean shutdown, passed to awatch() stop_event param; (3) Added _watch_path() method - returns log file path if exists, parent directory otherwise; (4) Added _make_watch_filter() method - filter function to only react to the specific log file when watching parent dir; (5) Replaced start() sleep loop with _watch_loop() using awatch() which uses inotify/kqueue/FSEvents under the hood. Updated tests: added 6 new tests for event-driven behavior (_watch_path, _make_watch_filter, stop_event, and integration tests), removed poll_interval arg from existing tests.

comments.author: oompah
comments.text: Verification: All 52 tests in test_error_watcher.py pass. The 12 pre-existing failures in other test files (test_collapsed_epics.py, test_draft_epic_swimlane.py, test_focus.py) are unrelated to our changes and existed before. PR created: https://github.com/lesserevil/oompah/pull/37

comments.author: oompah
comments.text: Completion: Replaced polling with event-driven file watching in LogFileWatcher (oompah/error_watcher.py). The asyncio.sleep()-based poll loop is gone; watchfiles.awatch() (inotify/kqueue/FSEvents) now drives file change detection. The watcher handles: files that don't exist yet (watches parent dir), log rotation, graceful shutdown via asyncio.Event. 52 tests pass. PR: https://github.com/lesserevil/oompah/pull/37

title: Implement event-driven infrastructure
description: Set up an event-driven system within the app
close_reason: Implemented EventBus in oompah/events.py with 13 typed event types, sync+async dispatch, error isolation, and idempotent subscription. Wired into Orchestrator at all lifecycle points. 41 tests added, all passing. PR: https://github.com/lesserevil/oompah/pull/38

comments.author: oompah
comments.text: I understand the issue: This task is part of the 'All actions must be event driven' epic (oompah-ky3). The goal is to implement an internal event-driven infrastructure within the app — no external queuing systems. Currently, the orchestrator has ad-hoc observer patterns (_observers, _state_only_observers, _activity_observers lists). My plan: 1) Create an EventBus class in oompah/events.py with typed events, subscribe/emit/unsubscribe, and async support. 2) Define a set of standard event types (agent_dispatched, agent_completed, agent_failed, issue_state_changed, orchestrator_paused, etc.). 3) Wire the orchestrator to use EventBus internally instead of direct observer list mutations. 4) Write comprehensive unit tests in tests/test_events.py. This keeps all event handling in-app with no external dependencies.

comments.author: oompah
comments.text: Discovery: The current observer pattern uses three raw lists (_observers, _state_only_observers, _activity_observers) in the orchestrator with direct callbacks. The server.py registers callbacks by appending to these lists. This works but is ad-hoc — there's no event typing, no ordering guarantees, no easy way to add new event types without modifying the observer interface. I'll implement a proper EventBus (in oompah/events.py) with: typed event constants, sync+async handler support, subscribe/unsubscribe, and error isolation per handler. The orchestrator will use the EventBus internally while maintaining backward compatibility via the existing observer list interface.

comments.author: oompah
comments.text: Implementation complete. Created oompah/events.py with EventBus class (typed EventType enum, sync emit(), async emit_async(), subscribe/unsubscribe/unsubscribe_all, error isolation per handler, idempotent subscription). Integrated EventBus into Orchestrator: added event_bus attribute, emits AGENT_DISPATCHED on dispatch, ORCHESTRATOR_PAUSED/RESUMED on pause/unpause, ORCHESTRATOR_TICK/_STATE_UPDATED/AGENT_ACTIVITY on every notify, AGENT_COMPLETED/FAILED/STALLED/MAX_TURNS on worker exit, ISSUE_RETRY_SCHEDULED on retry scheduling. title: Add Finalize/Mark-as-Draft toggle button to swimlane header
description:
In the swimlane view, add a toggle button in the swimlane header actions area. The button should show 'Finalize' for draft epics and 'Mark as Draft' for non-draft epics.

## Button Behavior

**For draft epics** (epic has 'draft' label):
- Button label: 'Finalize'
- On click: calls DELETE /api/v1/issues/{identifier}/labels/draft (removes 'draft' label), then calls PATCH /api/v1/issues/{identifier} with {status: 'deferred'} to set it to deferred
- After success: calls refreshBoard() to update the UI

**For non-draft epics** (epic does NOT have 'draft' label):
- Button label: 'Mark as Draft'
- On click: calls POST /api/v1/issues/{identifier}/labels with body {label: 'draft', project_id: ...}, then calls PATCH /api/v1/issues/{identifier} with {status: 'deferred'}
- After success: calls refreshBoard() to update the UI

## Implementation

1. In `renderSwimlaneView` (dashboard.html, around line 1299), inside the `.swimlane-actions` span, add the toggle button after the existing '+ Child' and 'Details' buttons.

2. Add a new async function `toggleEpicDraft(epicIdentifier, isDraft, projectId)` that:
   - If isDraft: DELETEs the 'draft' label, then PATCHes status to 'deferred'
   - If not isDraft: POSTs the 'draft' label, then PATCHes status to 'deferred'  
   - Uses the existing label API: POST /api/v1/issues/{id}/labels and DELETE /api/v1/issues/{id}/labels/draft
   - Calls refreshBoard() after completion
   - Handles errors gracefully (logs to console)

3. Wire the button in the swimlane header template:
   ```
   const isDraft = (epic.labels || []).includes('draft');
   const draftBtnLabel = isDraft ? 'Finalize' : 'Mark as Draft';
   // In the template:
   <button onclick="event.stopPropagation(); toggleEpicDraft('${esc(epic.identifier)}', ${isDraft}, '${esc(epic.project_id || '')}')">
     ${draftBtnLabel}
   </button>
   ```

## API Endpoints (already implemented in oompah-5e0)

- POST /api/v1/issues/{identifier}/labels  — body: {label, project_id}
- DELETE /api/v1/issues/{identifier}/labels/{label} — query param: project_id
- PATCH /api/v1/issues/{identifier} — body: {status, project_id}

## Tests

Add test class `TestSwimlaneDraftToggleButton` in `tests/test_draft_swimlane_toggle.py` covering:
- `toggleEpicDraft` function exists in the script
- For draft epics: 'Finalize' button is rendered in the swimlane actions
- For non-draft epics: 'Mark as Draft' button is rendered in the swimlane actions
- Button calls `toggleEpicDraft` with correct arguments including isDraft flag
- Button calls `event.stopPropagation()`
- `toggleEpicDraft` calls DELETE for the draft label when isDraft is true
- `toggleEpicDraft` calls POST for the draft label when isDraft is false
- `toggleEpicDraft` sets status to 'deferred' in both cases
- `toggleEpicDraft` calls refreshBoard after completion

## Context

- `renderSwimlaneView` is in `oompah/templates/dashboard.html` around line 1279
- The label API and PATCH endpoints are tested in `tests/test_server_label_api.py`
- Follow patterns of other API calls in the script (e.g., `updateIssue` function)

title: Add Draft badge to swimlane header for draft epics
description:
In the swimlane view (renderSwimlaneView function in dashboard.html), add a 'Draft' badge next to the epic title in the swimlane header when the epic has the 'draft' label.

## What to do

In the `renderSwimlaneView` function (around line 1295), inside the swimlane header `lane.innerHTML` template literal, add conditional badge HTML between the `swimlane-title` span and the `swimlane-counts` span. The badge should:

- Only appear when `(epic.labels || []).includes('draft')` is true
- Use a new CSS class `.swimlane-draft-badge` (distinct from `.draft-epic-badge` on cards)
- Display the text 'Draft'
- Have an `aria-label='Draft epic'`
- Use the blue/accent color scheme (consistent with `.draft-epic-badge`)

## CSS

Add a `.swimlane-draft-badge` CSS rule near the existing `.draft-epic-badge` rule (around line 335). It should use similar styling but may be slightly larger to match the swimlane header scale.

- `.swimlane-draft-badge` CSS class exists in the HTML
- Badge HTML is generated in `renderSwimlaneView` when epic has 'draft' label
- Badge is NOT generated when epic does not have 'draft' label
- Badge uses `.swimlane-draft-badge` CSS class
- Badge text is 'Draft'

## Context

- The label API endpoints already exist (POST/DELETE /api/v1/issues/{id}/labels)
- The `renderSwimlaneView` is in `oompah/templates/dashboard.html` around line 1279
- The draft badge on cards (`.draft-epic-badge`) is separate from this swimlane header badge
- See `tests/test_draft_epic_badge.py` for the pattern to follow

title: Draft Epic Planning Workflow
description: Add a 'draft' label for epics that makes them flow through the kanban like normal issues. Draft epics appear in the kanban board columns alongside tasks/bugs/features. An epic_planner focus picks up draft epics and breaks them into child tasks with dependencies. When planning is complete, the agent removes the draft label and sets the epic to deferred, at which point it becomes a normal epic container visible only in swimlane view. The swimlane view gets a toggle to add/remove the draft label.

title: Prevent duplicate projects in projects.json
description: Adding a project via the UI creates duplicate entries in .oompah/projects.json. The project-add endpoint must check for an existing project with the same repo_url before inserting. If a duplicate is found, reject the request or update the existing entry.

title: Fetching models from provider can scroll beyond the screen
description: When adding a provider and pressing the 'fetch models' button, if there are a large number of models, they can make the dialog so large it can expand beyond the screen. The multi-select for models should scroll when there are more models than will fit in the dialog.

comments.author: Shawn Edwards
comments.text: I understand the issue: When fetching models from a provider in the add provider dialog, if there are many models returned, the multi-select component expands the dialog beyond the screen boundaries. The fix needs to add scrolling to the model multi-select so it stays within the dialog/screen bounds. My plan is to explore the codebase to find the provider dialog component and add appropriate CSS constraints (max-height + overflow-y: scroll/auto) to the models multi-select.

comments.author: Shawn Edwards
comments.text: Found the relevant code: In server.py, the PROVIDERS_HTML contains the provider dialog. The models section uses a 'tag-input-wrap' div (class tag-input-wrap) that wraps model tags in a flex container. When many models are fetched, the tag-input-wrap div expands vertically without any height constraint, causing the dialog to grow beyond the screen. The fix is to add max-height and overflow-y: auto to the tag-input-wrap element so it scrolls when there are many models.

title: Agent-log popup: add Verbose toggle (off = show only human-readable message entries, no expand/collapse)
description:
Add a "Verbose" toggle to the agent-log popup (the modal opened by clicking a running-agent chip on the dashboard). The toggle controls how much detail is rendered:

- **Verbose: ON** (current behavior): every activity entry — tool_call, tool_result, message, permission_grant, acp_session_start, etc. — appears as a collapsed row with kind / turn / summary header and a click-to-expand body containing the full detail.

- **Verbose: OFF** (new): only entries whose `kind === 'message'` appear (or whichever kinds carry plain-text human-readable content — implementer should audit). Each is rendered with the full text always visible, no expand/collapse toggle, no kind/turn pills cluttering the header. Read like a transcript.

The use case: the operator wants a quick "what did the agent say" view without wading through every tool call. Verbose mode is for forensic debugging; the new compact mode is for normal observation.

# UI

Add a checkbox at the top of the activity-panel content area, immediately under the title bar. Label: "Verbose: ON" / "Verbose: OFF" depending on state, mirroring the existing "In-flight only" toggle pattern on the main dashboard.

Default: ON (preserve current behavior on first open).
Persistence: localStorage key `oompah_agent_log_verbose` ('on' | 'off'), so per-operator-per-machine preference sticks.

# Implementation outline

In `renderActivityEntry(a)` at oompah/templates/dashboard.html:2211:
- Read `isAgentLogVerbose()` (similar pattern to `isHideMergedOn()`).
- If verbose=true: existing behavior unchanged.
- If verbose=false:
  - Skip rendering entries whose `kind` is NOT in the "human-message" allowlist. Initial allowlist: `['message']`. Implementer extends if other kinds carry user-readable prose.
  - For kept entries, render a simpler `<div>` with the summary text only — no header pills, no toggle, no `expanded` class manipulation, no `<span class="activity-toggle">`.

Toggle handler `toggleAgentLogVerbose()`:
- Persist to localStorage
- Re-render the activity panel by clearing the body and replaying the cached activity entries (they should already be in memory; if not, re-fetch via the existing refreshActivity path).

# Acceptance criteria

- Checkbox renders at top of agent-log popup with current state visible in the label ("Verbose: ON" / "Verbose: OFF").
- Toggling: instantly re-renders the visible entries; no flicker, no extra network call.
- Verbose OFF mode hides tool_call / tool_result / permission_grant / acp_* / etc. entries; only message-kind entries are visible.
- In Verbose OFF mode, message entries show full text inline; no expand/collapse interaction.
- Verbose ON mode preserves today's behavior exactly (regression check).
- localStorage persistence: closing and reopening the popup preserves the toggle state.
- Live push: when a new entry arrives via `handleActivityPush` while the panel is open, it's filtered/rendered per the current toggle state.

# Out of scope

- Filtering by other criteria (per-tool, per-turn-range, search).
- Server-side filtering — keep this purely render-time so the WebSocket push payload is unchanged.
- Migrating any other activity-panel UI elements (kind pills, turn numbers, etc.) — Verbose ON keeps everything as-is.

# Open question for the implementer

What's the full list of "message-kind-like" entries that should remain visible in Verbose OFF mode? Initial proposal: `['message']`. May need to also include some flavors of `text` or `assistant_message` depending on what oompah actually emits — suggest auditing a recent agent log file (e.g. `~/.oompah/agent-logs/*.jsonl`) and listing distinct `kind` values that carry human-readable prose.

comments.author: Shawn Edwards
comments.text: Updated requirement: default state is **OFF** (compact, message-only view). Operators who want the full forensic view can flip the toggle to ON; that preference persists in localStorage for return visits. The first-open default applies only to operators who haven't yet set the toggle.

comments.author: oompah
comments.text:
Understanding: Add a 'Verbose' toggle (checkbox) inside the agent activity overlay (oompah/templates/dashboard.html, activity-header area). Default ON, persisted in localStorage key 'oompah_agent_log_verbose'. When OFF, renderActivityEntry() returns null for non-message kinds and renders a minimal text-only div for message-kind entries (no header pills, no expand/collapse). Toggle re-renders the panel from a cached entries array.

Audit of activity kinds (orchestrator's kind_map + api_agent _emit calls): message, thinking, tool_call, tool_result, error, warning. Allowlist will be ['message'] per the issue's initial proposal — that covers acp_text (real model speech), acp_session_start, acp_result, and the api_agent message-kind emits ('Agent finished', 'Task cancelled', etc.). All other kinds are forensic tool/permission noise.

Implementation plan:
1. Add CSS for .activity-verbose-bar and .activity-entry-compact.
2. Add toolbar div under activity-header with checkbox.
3. Add isAgentLogVerbose() / toggleAgentLogVerbose() / setAgentLogVerboseLabel() helpers.
4. Cache the entries array (window._currentActivityEntries) in refreshActivity and append to it in handleActivityPush, so toggle can re-render without a network call.
5. Modify renderActivityEntry: if !verbose && kind !== 'message' → return null; if !verbose && kind === 'message' → return compact div.
6. Modify refreshActivity / handleActivityPush to handle null returns.
7. Update openActivityPanel to call init for the toggle.

title: Reviews UI: 'blocked' label is misleading for queued PRs (rule 3 in deriveQueueStatus needs to include mergeable_state='blocked')
description:
UI bug in reviews.html's `deriveQueueStatus` function: when a PR has `auto_merge_enabled=True` AND `mergeable_state='blocked'`, it falls through to the catch-all rule 8 and renders the raw label "blocked" — which looks like an actionable problem state.

In reality, this combination almost always means "PR is sitting in the merge queue while GitHub processes the queue's own required checks." mergeable_state='blocked' on a queued PR is GitHub's way of saying "you can't directly merge this — the queue is handling it." It's the **default state of a queued PR** until the queue's synthetic merge_group commit passes CI.

# Live evidence on 2026-05-08

All 5 open trickle PRs (#34, #46, #56, #57, #58) showed `auto_merge=True` + `mergeable_state='blocked'` in oompah's cache. The merge-queue GraphQL query confirmed all 5 are in the queue (positions 1-5). They're not "blocked" in any actionable sense — they're just queued and waiting. The UI rendered them as "blocked" anyway, prompting the operator to ask "what's wrong with these?"

# Fix

In `oompah/templates/reviews.html` `deriveQueueStatus` (around line 334), extend rule 3 to include `'blocked'`:

```javascript
// 3. Already accepted into the GitHub merge queue.
if (ame && (ms === 'clean' || ms === 'unstable' || ms === 'behind' || ms === '' || ms === 'unknown' || ms === 'blocked')) {
  return {label: 'in queue', cls: 'queue-in-queue', emoji: '⏳', sortRank: 5};
}
```

The 'blocked' alias for "in queue" is the same shape as the existing 'unknown' alias added for the same reason — both are GitHub-internal states that, combined with auto_merge=True, mean "the queue has it, just be patient."

Rule 6's existing `!ame && ms === 'blocked'` → "blocked: needs config" stays as-is. That's the genuinely actionable case (allow_auto_merge is off or required checks missing on a non-queued PR).

# Acceptance criteria

- A PR with `auto_merge_enabled=True, mergeable_state='blocked'` renders as "in queue" with the existing queue-in-queue style.
- A PR with `auto_merge_enabled=False, mergeable_state='blocked'` still renders as "blocked: needs config" (rule 6 unchanged — that's a legitimate operator-action case).
- All other combinations unchanged.
- Tests in tests/test_dashboard*.html-related coverage (or extend `tests/test_dashboard_hide_merged.py`'s style of static-pattern testing): assert the new keyword appears in the rule 3 condition; assert no behavior change for the `!ame` blocked case.

# Out of scope

- Rule reordering or LLM-based status inference. This is purely the additive label fix.
- Backend caching of mergeable_state — already addressed by the earlier 'unknown' fix; same caching path applies.

Companion to the earlier render-time fix that aliased 'unknown' to "in queue" for queued PRs.

title: Nested-epic merge chain in shared mode: child epic completion targets parent epic's branch, not main
description:
amd shipped the `epic_strategy` setting (flat / stacked / shared) with one-level epic→child support. Nested epics are partially handled today: each epic level is a self-contained shared boundary (children of nested epic B share B's worktree), but the merge-chain case isn't. When B completes, the orchestrator pushes B's branch and opens a PR targeting `main` — not targeting A's branch (where A is B's parent epic).

Net effect: nested epics work as independent boundaries. They don't give you Linux-kernel-style multi-level merge trains where:

  Tasks under B → land on B's branch → B's "completion PR" merges B's branch into A's branch → tasks-direct-under-A continue accumulating on A's branch → A's "completion PR" merges A into main when ALL of A's epic-children plus any direct task children have landed.

# When this matters

- Operator structures a large feature as Epic A with sub-epics B, C, D and a few direct tasks. Today: each sub-epic lands to main as a separate PR, defeating the "single coherent landing per A" guarantee that shared mode otherwise gives.
- Multi-author handoff between teams where A is the umbrella and B/C/D are owned by sub-teams. Each sub-team's work lands when their sub-epic completes, but operator wants the umbrella feature to land all-or-nothing.

# When this DOESN'T matter (do nothing)

- Single-level epics with task children only — already covered by amd.
- Cases where each epic is genuinely independent and just happens to be in a hierarchical bd structure for organization. Today's behavior is correct here.

# Design sketch

In `_push_epic_branch` (orchestrator.py:1449) and the corresponding completion-detection logic (`_auto_close_completed_epics` etc.):

1. **Target branch resolution** — when about to push an epic-completion PR for epic E, check if E has a parent epic P. If yes, target P's branch instead of `main`. If no, keep targeting `main` as today.

2. **Wait for siblings** — when computing "all children terminal" for epic E, the existing logic already does that. No change. But for epic A whose direct children include both other epics (B, C, D) AND tasks: the auto-close trigger for A still requires ALL direct children terminal, which already covers nested epics correctly.

3. **Top-of-chain push** — when the top-level epic (no parent of its own) reaches completion AND all its descendants have landed onto its branch via the chain, push to main as one PR.

4. **Project setting awareness** — only kick in when `epic_strategy='shared'`. For `flat` mode, ignore (today's behavior). For `stacked` mode, the chain is a no-op anyway (each child PR already targets the parent epic's branch directly).

# Concrete code changes

```python
# in _push_epic_branch:
def _resolve_target_branch(self, epic: Issue) -> str:
    parent_epic = self._resolve_parent_epic(epic)
    if parent_epic is None:
        return "main"
    return self._epic_branch_name(parent_epic.identifier)
```

Then `gh pr create --base <_resolve_target_branch(epic)>` instead of always `--base main`.

# Acceptance criteria

- A → (B → tasks) → main: when all tasks under B land, B's completion PR targets A's branch (NOT main). When A's completion fires (all direct children terminal), A's PR targets main.
- A → (B → tasks AND direct tasks under A): A's auto-close still requires both B AND its direct tasks to be terminal. A's PR targets main.
- Three-level nesting A → B → C → tasks: C → B's branch; B → A's branch; A → main. Each level fires on its own children's terminal-state.
- `flat` and `stacked` modes: unchanged. New behavior only activates when project's `epic_strategy='shared'` (and the epic in question has a parent epic).
- Tests in tests/test_epic_strategy.py extend with multi-level fixtures.
- Live: operator constructs a 2-level nested epic with shared mode; observe one main-targeted PR (the top epic's), not three.

# Out of scope

- Cross-project epic chains (epic in project X is parent of epic in project Y). Out of scope; today projects are independent units.
- Stacked-mode multi-level chaining. Stacked already works because each child PR explicitly targets the parent epic's branch — there's no "completion PR" intermediate step. So stacked nests naturally; this bead is shared-mode-only.
- Migration of existing in-flight nested epics to the new behavior. Operator restarts / re-creates the worktree if they want to switch.

# Risks

- More complex completion-detection logic. A bug here could leave epics open forever (tasks land but the chain doesn't complete) or close them prematurely.
- Force-pushes on the parent epic's branch: when a child epic merges into the parent epic's branch, that's a regular merge. Force-push only happens within a single epic's worktree (rebase-on-update). No new force-push pattern.

# Companion to

- oompah-zlz_2-amd (the parent feature; this is a follow-up extension)

title: Focus override: LLM triage prompt includes recent operator corrections as few-shot
description:
Child C of the focus-override epic. Consumes override events to inform LLM triage. Sibling of child B (deterministic scorer learns); together they cover both triage paths.

Depends on child A landing first.

# Behavior

In oompah/focus.py's `_build_triage_prompt(issue, foci)`:
- Fetch the most recent N override events (default N=10) for this project from the override-history store.
- If any exist, append a "Operator corrections" section to the prompt, formatted as:

```
## Operator corrections (recent overrides)

The operator has previously overridden triage decisions on similar issues. Take these into account.

- On issue 'CI-Speed 3: build-once + artifact reuse...' (labels=[ci-fix], type=task), operator chose focus=ci_fix instead of triage-suggested devops. Reason: "this is a ci-fix bead, not devops infrastructure work."

- On issue 'Resolve merge conflicts on PR #30 (trickle-c0w...)' (labels=[merge-conflict], type=task), operator chose focus=merge_conflict instead of triage-suggested queue_api. Reason: "merge-conflict specialist has the right rails."
```

- Truncate to most recent N events to keep prompt size bounded.
- Prepend these AFTER the existing focus-list-and-issue section but BEFORE the LLM's "respond with focus_name: reasoning" instruction.

# Why not just "always boost the override focus's deterministic score"?

That's child B. C provides the LLM-path equivalent: the LLM can match patterns (semantic similarity, intent inferred from title/description) that deterministic scoring can't catch. Together they double up.

# Bounded prompt size

Each override entry is ~300-500 tokens. With N=10 default that's ~3-5K tokens added to the triage prompt. Acceptable for the small models used in triage. If prompt size becomes an issue, tune N down or summarize older entries.

# Acceptance criteria

- _build_triage_prompt with override history available emits the "Operator corrections" section in the right place.
- _build_triage_prompt with no overrides for the project produces today's prompt unchanged (no empty section).
- LLM triage with override history shows higher accuracy on test fixtures designed to look like the overridden patterns (manual / fixture-based eval, not automated A/B).
- Sanity-check (existing): if the LLM picks a focus whose deterministic score is 0, fall back as before. The new "Operator corrections" section should make hallucinations rarer, not enable new ones.
- Tests in tests/test_focus_triage.py: assert section appears in the prompt when history exists; assert order; assert truncation at N events.
- Live verification: with B already shipped (deterministic boost), confirm that after a few overrides, both deterministic AND LLM agree on the correct focus for the next similar issue.

# Out of scope

- Cross-project override sharing in the prompt — keep per-project for now.
- Adaptive N (more events for cold-start projects, fewer for projects with strong patterns) — tune-it-later concern.
- A "feedback signal" back from triage → LLM training; we're not fine-tuning, just shaping the prompt.

# Companion

- Sibling of child B (deterministic scorer learns from overrides). Land in either order.

title: Focus override: deterministic score_focus consumes override history (boost / dampen)
description:
Child B of the focus-override epic. Consumes the override events persisted by child A; modifies the deterministic score_focus path so future similar issues are routed correctly without re-overriding.

Depends on child A landing first.

# Behavior

In oompah/focus.py's `score_focus(focus, issue)`:
- After the existing scoring (keyword / label / type / priority), look up the override-history store (via the API child A added).
- For each override event in the last <window> (recommendation: 30 days):
  - If the historical issue is "similar" to `issue` (matching algorithm below), and the operator chose `override_focus == focus.name`: boost score by +20.
  - If the historical issue is similar AND the LLM/scorer chose `original_focus == focus.name` AND operator overrode away: dampen score by -20.

Net effect: foci the operator has historically chosen for similar-shaped issues win the deterministic score. Foci the operator has historically rejected lose ground.

# Similarity matching

Start simple, iterate later if needed:
- Same project_id.
- Same issue_type.
- At least one shared label (case-insensitive).

If both conditions match, treat as "similar." This is a deliberate floor — operator overrides are rare enough that we want broad pattern match, not narrow exact-match.

Window: 30 days OR last 50 events, whichever is broader. Configurable via ServiceConfig.focus_override_window_days (default 30) and focus_override_window_count (default 50).

# Why deterministic boost, not absolute override

The deterministic scorer is one of two paths (LLM is the other). If we hard-flip the deterministic result based on a single override, we lose the LLM's actual signal. +20 is enough to swing a tie or close-call but not enough to flip a decisive label-match win. Tune empirically.

# Acceptance criteria

- After 1 override on a similar issue, score_focus's output for the next similar issue shows the override target gaining +20 and the original losing -20 (verifiable via score_focus return values in tests).
- After 5 overrides on the same direction, the override target wins deterministic scoring outright on similar issues.
- An override of "A → B" on issue X does NOT influence routing for an issue Y in a different project.
- An override on issue X with labels [foo, bar] does NOT influence routing for an issue with labels [baz] (no shared label = not similar).
- Tests in tests/test_focus.py: extend with override-history fixtures and assert score deltas.
- Live verification: produce a triage mistake on a fresh issue, override, then re-trigger triage on a similar fresh issue and observe correct routing without operator action.

# Out of scope (child C)

- LLM triage's use of override events.
- Decay function over time (a single weight for "last N days" is fine for this iteration; per-event time decay is a refinement).
- Operator-facing UI to view / clear override history (CLI / DB query is sufficient for now).

# Companion

- Sibling of child C (LLM triage). They land independently and either alone delivers value.

comments.author: oompah
comments.text:
Understanding: Modify oompah/focus.py's score_focus(focus, issue) to consume override-history events persisted by child A (focus-override-* bd memories). After existing keyword/label/type/priority scoring, apply +20 boost to events where override_focus matches focus.name on a 'similar' historical issue, and -20 dampen where original_focus matches but operator overrode away. Similarity = same project_id + same issue_type + ≥1 shared label (case-insensitive). Window = 30 days OR last 50 events, whichever broader, configurable via ServiceConfig.focus_override_window_days/_count.

Plan:
1. Rebase z22 on origin/oompah-zlz_2-8yt (done — child A's branch wasn't in main yet).
2. Add focus_override_window_days/_count to ServiceConfig.
3. Add load_override_history(tracker, *, project_id=None, window_days=30, window_count=50) helper to focus.py that reads bd memories with focus-override-* keys and parses JSON values, applying the window.
4. Add _is_similar_issue(issue, event) similarity check.
5. Extend score_focus signature with optional override_history kwarg; apply +20/-20 deltas.
6. Plumb override_history through select_focus / select_focus_async (optional kwarg, default None to keep existing tests passing).
7. Wire orchestrator dispatch path to load history once and pass to focus selection.
8. Tests in tests/test_focus.py exercising all acceptance criteria (1 override, 5 overrides, project isolation, label disjointness, window expiry).

title: [epic] Operator focus override + triage learning loop
description:
[epic] Operator focus override + triage learning loop.

Today the focus for a dispatched agent is chosen by select_focus_with_llm (LLM triage) or score_focus (deterministic), with no operator override path. When triage picks the wrong focus (devops instead of ci_fix; queue_api instead of merge_conflict), the operator's only recourse today is to wait for the agent to bounce, kill the dispatch indirectly, or relabel the bead and hope.

Worse, the triage system has no feedback channel — bad triage decisions repeat. The 0pr / 2sd carve-outs for ci-fix and merge-conflict were band-aids over the same underlying problem: triage doesn't learn from operator corrections.

# Goal

1. Operator can override the focus on a running agent from the agent-log popup. If the change requires restart, restart is fine.
2. Override events feed back into triage so future similar issues are routed correctly without operator intervention.

# Deliverables (children)

- **A. UI + endpoint + persist override event** (P1). Adds a focus selector to the agent-log popup. POST /api/v1/agents/{identifier}/override-focus kills the running agent and re-dispatches with the chosen focus. Persists the override event (issue snapshot + original focus + override focus + timestamp) to a queryable store. Delivers immediate UX value even before B/C land.

- **B. Deterministic triage uses override history** (P2, depends on A). When score_focus runs, it consults the override-event store. For each prior override "A → B" on a similar issue (matching keywords / labels / type within a configurable window), boost B's score and dampen A's. The signal needs to be strong enough to flip a clear LLM mistake but not so strong that one override permanently pins routing for unrelated issues.

- **C. LLM triage uses override history** (P2, depends on A). select_focus_with_llm includes the most recent N override events as few-shot examples in the prompt. Format: "on issue '<title>' (labels=[...], type=...), operator chose focus=<X> instead of triage-suggested <Y> with reasoning '<reasoning>'." This is exactly the pattern LLMs handle well — pattern-match from operator corrections.

# Acceptance for the epic (each child has its own acceptance criteria)

- A operator can correct a misrouted agent in <30 seconds via the dashboard with no terminal commands.
- After ~5 overrides on similar issues, both deterministic and LLM triage independently route the next similar issue correctly without operator intervention.
- Override history is queryable, auditable, and survives oompah restarts.

# Why epic, not single bead

- Each child can land independently and ship value: A unlocks UX immediately; B and C provide compounding triage improvement on top.
- A and {B,C} have different risk profiles. A is mechanical (UI + endpoint + persistence). B and C touch the focus-selection logic where regressions are visible and high-impact.
- Code review is more focused per-PR.

# Open design questions (resolve in child A)

- Storage substrate: bd memories (already cross-session, queryable) vs new file under .oompah/. Recommendation: bd memories with a stable `--key=focus-override-{issue-id}` key, body containing the structured snapshot.
- Override event "similarity" matching for B: keyword overlap? Label match? Issue-type? Recommendation: start with exact-label-match + issue-type-match; iterate.
- Window: how far back does override history influence triage? Recommendation: last 30 days OR last 50 events (whichever bigger), with explicit "expire" controls per event.

# Out of scope (explicit)

- Cross-project override sharing — overrides apply per-project. An operator's preference on `oompah` doesn't bleed into `trickle`.
- Mid-run focus switching without restart — too complex for the agent SDK's session model. Restart is acceptable.
- Auto-rollback if the operator-chosen focus also fails — separate concern (escalation hierarchy).
- Multi-operator weighting — single-operator setup for now; multi-operator can layer on if/when needed.

title: Per-project epic_strategy setting: flat / stacked / shared (controls how epic children relate to branches and CI)
description:
Per-project setting controlling how children of an epic relate to branches and CI. Three modes; default preserves current behavior.

# Modes

## flat (default — current behavior)
- Every bead, including epic children, gets its own worktree off main
- Each PR targets main
- CI runs per child PR
- Merge queue serializes the merges to main

## stacked
- Every bead gets its own worktree, but children of an epic create their PR against the epic's branch (not main)
- Epic owns a long-lived branch on origin
- CI runs on each child PR (against the epic branch)
- When all children land into the epic branch, the orchestrator pushes the epic branch and opens an epic→main PR; CI runs again on that PR
- Merge to main is one combined PR per epic

## shared
- Each epic gets ONE worktree and ONE branch, shared by all of its child beads
- Children commit directly to the epic's branch — no per-child PRs, no per-child CI
- CI only runs when the orchestrator opens the epic→main PR
- Child beads within the same epic dispatch SERIALLY (one agent per epic-worktree at a time); multiple epics still dispatch in parallel up to max_in_flight_prs
- Trade: loses intra-epic parallelism in exchange for fewer merges, fewer CI runs, no inter-child conflicts on main

# Project setting

Add to projects.json schema:

  "epic_strategy": "flat" | "stacked" | "shared"   (default: "flat")

Surface in /projects-manage UI as a radio group with the trade-off described inline.

# Orchestrator changes

## Worktree allocation
In _dispatch, before creating a new worktree:
  if project.epic_strategy in ("shared",) and issue.parent_epic_id:
      epic_worktree = self._get_or_create_epic_worktree(project, epic_id)
      use epic_worktree (and the epic's branch) for this child
  else:
      existing per-bead worktree path

## Child dispatch serialization (shared mode)
When epic_strategy=="shared", _should_dispatch must reject any child whose epic already has an in-flight child agent. Idea: maintain epic_in_flight_count; gate same-epic dispatch when count >= 1.

## Child PR target (stacked mode)
When epic_strategy=="stacked", child PRs created via provider.open_pull_request() get base_branch=epic.branch_name instead of main. The orchestrator already knows the epic from the child's parent-child dependency.

## Epic completion + PR creation (stacked + shared)
Triggered after every reconcile pass (cheap — uses cached candidates):
  for epic in candidates:
      if not has children: continue
      if any child is open / in_progress: continue
      if any child is NOT closed (e.g. deferred, blocked): continue
      if epic already has an open PR: continue
      # All children done — push the epic branch and open the epic→main PR.
      provider.push_branch(epic_branch)
      provider.open_pull_request(epic_branch -> main, title=epic.title, body=epic.description)

## Epic completion: edge cases
- Child reopens after a YOLO retry: epic completion check must observe the new in_progress and NOT trigger epic PR creation. Mid-flight children DELAY the push.
- Child closes without merging (e.g. rejected by reviewer in stacked mode): in stacked, epic branch is missing that child's commits; in shared, the commits are already on the epic branch directly. Stacked mode needs to either fail-loudly or treat this as "epic incomplete, await operator decision".
- Epic-planner adds children mid-flight: those new children must complete before epic PR creation. Already covered by "any child not closed → wait" rule.

# Acceptance criteria
- New `epic_strategy` field on Project model with backward-compatible default
- /projects-manage edit form exposes the setting; back-compat reads existing projects.json without the field
- Mode `flat` produces today's behavior with zero change to dispatch / worktree / PR creation
- Mode `stacked`:
  - Child PRs target the epic's branch (verified via provider.open_pull_request call)
  - Epic→main PR opens automatically when all children close
  - Worktrees remain per-bead; child dispatch unchanged from flat (parallel up to cap)
- Mode `shared`:
  - All children of an epic share one worktree at a path like ~/.oompah/worktrees/{project}/epic-{epic_id}
  - Only one child of a given epic can be in_progress at a time
  - No child PR is opened; the epic's PR is the only one
  - Epic→main PR opens automatically when all children close
  - Multiple epics can dispatch concurrently (cap is per-project, not per-epic)
- Tests cover all three modes for: dispatch gating, worktree allocation, PR target, epic completion trigger
- Migration: existing projects default to `flat` (no DB migration needed beyond the default)

# Out of scope (separate beads)
- Coordination protocol so multiple agents can work concurrently in the same epic worktree (the user explicitly accepted serial-within-epic for this iteration)
- Auto-detection of "this should be an epic" from bead structure
- Stacked-mode handling when the merge queue is enabled on the project (CI on the merge_group commit may need different handling than CI on the epic branch directly — investigate before stacked-mode lands on merge-queue projects)

Plan: (1) add Project.epic_strategy field in models.py with default 'flat', (2) update /projects-manage UI to expose radio group, (3) orchestrator changes for worktree allocation (shared), dispatch gating (shared serial), PR base branch (stacked), epic completion + PR creation (stacked+shared), (4) tests across all three modes.

title: Per-project test_command config so agents don't have to guess the right test target
description:
Right now, when an agent (especially merge_conflict) needs to verify a rebase before push, it picks a test command by inference. The merge_conflict focus says 'Run tests after resolving all conflicts to verify nothing is broken' but prescribes no specific command. The default profile says even less.

Concrete failure mode observed on trickle-dhr (2026-05-06):
- Agent picked 'cargo test --workspace --no-fail-fast'
- This is the broadest target in a Rust cargo workspace, pulling in EVERY crate
- For trickle, that includes trickle-native (platform-specific signaling), trickle-iroh-turn (network), trickle-web (browser-targeted), trickle-server (cross-platform)
- The actual run was harmless — most crates have ~0 unit tests, completed <1s — but ONLY because the workspace is currently test-light. Once test coverage grows, --workspace will:
  * Run cross-platform tests on whatever local OS the agent is on
  * Hit network-dependent paths (iroh, signaling) that may flake or require fixtures
  * Run browser/wasm tests if those get unit coverage
  * Take far longer than the agent expects, possibly timing out

Better design: each project declares its 'pre-push verification' command(s) once. Agents read it from the project config rather than guessing.

Schema sketch (in projects.json / projects.py):
  - test_command: e.g. 'cargo test --workspace --lib' for unit tests
  - test_command_full: optional, used pre-merge-queue when broader coverage matters
  - test_skip_paths: optional glob list to exclude flaky/hardware/network tests

UI: add fields to /projects edit form. Default behavior when unset: agent infers as today (back-compat).

Agent integration:
- merge_conflict focus prompt: include the project test_command as the test step (must_do becomes 'Run <test_command> and ensure all tests pass')
- Other focuses: optional reference in their must_do steps
- Agent prompt template (oompah/agent.py / api_agent.py / acp_agent.py) injects test_command into context

Cost: tiny (one config field, one prompt-template substitution).

Out of scope for this bead:
- Auto-detecting test_command from repo (parsing Cargo.toml workspace config, package.json scripts, etc) — could be a follow-up if explicit config is too high-friction
- Multi-stage test commands (smoke, full, integration) — start with one, expand later

Filed because the user noticed trickle-dhr running 'cargo test --workspace' and asked 'is that the right test target?' — and there's currently no way to TELL oompah what the right target is without per-bead notes.

7. Surface test_command in the WORKFLOW.md prompt template (Test Requirements section) so all focuses see it.
8. Add unit tests in test_projects_crud.py, test_prompt.py, and test_focus.py.

Skipping out-of-scope: auto-detection from Cargo.toml, multi-stage commands. Will keep test_skip_paths out for now since the issue lists it as 'optional' — start minimal.
